sentences.text: Yeah, well, the gentleman said they will show you all the phones.
He can disconnect the ones that have-- in the other-- far away, you know.
And these that are close, here.
You can--
Correction-- survivor's name is Ludmila Page.
The interviewer is Branko Lustig; the city, Beverly Hills, California; the language, English.
OK.
I-- my name is Ludmila Page; in Poland, it was Pfefferberg.
And I was born in Kishinev, Romania, on July 15, 1920.
Levinson.
Yes.
Yeah, the life in Kishinev I don't remember at all, because my parents apparently met during the first world war.
They both were doctors, and they met.
Romania-- that part of Romania was under Russian rule, at the time, and they both worked in the same hospital.
My father came-- my mother was a Russian from Kiev, and my father was from Poland, from the city of Lodz.
But they met in that hospital in-- I don't know if it was-- in those days, I was not-- when I was young, I was not interested to ask questions about-- [LAUGHS] --you know.
Now I would like to know so many more things.
But it's too late to ask those questions now.
And when I was a year old, my father brought my mother and myself from Romania, from Kishinev, to Lodz.
And this is where I lived all my life until the war broke, in 1939.
He was also a doctor.
My mother was a dermatologist; my father was a surgeon.
We-- well, we had an apartment.
You know, very few people, in those days-- we lived in a very nice apartment.
And I had a wonderful, wonderful childhood, in spite of-- of how many people describe their childhood in Poland.
I had a wonderful childhood.
I graduated high school in 1937.
And then, I went in November of 1937, I went to Vienna to-- to-- I was supposed to study medicine.
And the only thing-- I don't know why my mother insisted that I go to Vienna.
I hardly spoke German, so I had to improve my German.
[LAUGHS] I enrolled already in medical school, and then I had to work hard on improving my German.
But in March of 1938, Austria was annexed to Germany.
And by coincidence, our whole group of Polish students were supposed to go back to Poland for Easter vacation.
And we didn't know whether we will come back or not, because we witnessed the March of the Germans on March 13th-- 11 or 13th-- I don't remember exactly.
I-- not knowing anything-- I had a date with a friend on Opernplatz, in Vienna, which is the center of the city.
And I went there, and I saw the parade of the Germans.
And I saw how the Austrians were throwing flowers under their feet, and the enthusiasm of the local population with the arrival of the Germans.
So no one can tell me that Austria was occupied by Germany.
This was done very willingly.
This is the first--
The danger.
But, you see, being-- in 1938, I was-- I was 18 years old.
And I didn't realize.
You know, when I came back home, I should have warned my mother.
My father died before the war, in 1934.
No, I was an only child, yeah.
So I should have warned my mother, you know?
But we never expected that something like this could happen to our country-- to Poland, you know?
We believed in our army, and we thought that our cavalry can defeat the German tanks, you know?
It was naive-- very naive.
Personally, never.
I went to a school, to a Polish school, where we had mixed students-- gentile and-- and Jewish.
And we always got along fine.
There were, I knew-- I was aware of the fact that there were some right-wing organizations.
That began, I think with the advent of the Nazis in Germany.
I think this is when it happened-- after the death of Marshal Pilsudski.
This was about 1934, '35.
There were some right-wing organizations which were very anti-Semitic.
And also, the government had some decrees, which were also anti-Semitic in nature.
But on the whole, we lived a beautiful life.
We didn't really-- it was not that bad.
You know what I mean?
Describe life in your family before your father died and whether they were religious-- you-- you went to the synagogue?
My family-- we were-- my parents were very good Jews, you know?
For their holy days, they always went to the synagogue.
But otherwise, we were very, very assimilated, you know?
We-- first of all, we considered-- we-- we considered Jewishness as a religion, but we considered ourselves as very patriotic Poles, you know?
This was-- so I was brought up in a very assimilated milieu, you know?
Were the friends of your father-- you had some families in-- you had nobody in--
I-- my father's family moved to France.
My grandmother, my-- my father's brother and sister-- they all lived in France for a while-- I mean, until they died.
They died natural death.
Within six weeks, my father lost his entire family.
And my mother, being from Kiev, she had six brothers.
And she lost contact with everybody, because the relation with the Soviet Russia, at that time, was very, you know, difficult.
It was difficult to write and to receive letters.
So she really lost contact with her parents and-- and her brothers.
Oh, we had many friends.
And also, like I said, my parents had also mixed friends-- Polish.
My father, in his work, he worked in a-- a hospital which was a Catholic hospital, where Poland-- most of the institutions were Catholic, you know.
He had many friends, and I remember them exactly.
He associated with many friends who were non-Jewish.
Vienna.
No, no, I stayed in Lodz.
And not knowing what will happen-- but I knew one thing-- that we were not-- our whole group-- we will not go back to Vienna.
Because we read in the papers-- we heard the news and everything.
We knew what's going on there.
As a matter of fact, in Vienna, I already experienced the first atrocities of the Germans.
Because, when we boarded the train, you know, to go, and the train was still at the station in Vienna, the whole group of SS came on the train, examining people's documents.
And they behaved like wild animals-- pushing, shoving.
But we showed our Polish passports.
And at that time, they still respected the Polish passports.
So they left us alone, you know?
And we heard of the incidents of them attacking the Jews on the streets in the very first day after their arrival.
Because we left two days or three days after their arrival, and they already started all this behavior.
I review this moment, because you said you were a group of Polish students in Vienna.
On the university in Vienna, you had some feelings that something is happening to the Jews?
Absolutely not-- absolutely not.
It was-- this was-- we knew, because there was a plebiscite in Austria.
I don't remember when it was.
And the chancellor, Schuschnigg-- I can't remember exactly the politics of that time, but I know that-- I think the plebiscite showed that Austria population favored the annexation to Germany.
I applied to the University of Geneva, and I was accepted.
I was supposed to start in September of 1939-- the new semester.
And this was the end of my education.
When the war started, we were-- there were many restrictions right away.
You know, we lived on the main street.
Being that my parents were doctors, we had to live in a area which was accessible to-- easily accessible.
We lived-- so they-- they closed that street.
We couldn't walk on that street anymore.
We-- we-- luckily, we had some back exits, you know?
And we-- in order to go on the other side of the street, we had to go almost around the whole city.
We had to wear the yellow armbands-- excuse me.
My-- we-- you know, my mother started to prepare all the foods, you know, just in case.
And we also had a-- a gas attack.
I mean, we didn't have it, but it was a panic, terrible panic, and everybody was issued a gas masks and so on.
And the life was very, very difficult.
We lost contact with friends, because we couldn't walk freely.
You know, we just could walk in a certain areas of the city.
And then, in--
Yeah, I'm sorry.
By the Germans.
They, you know-- immediately.
After they walked in-- into our city, the rules were, you know-- this was the occupation rules, all over Poland, not only in our city.
No, no, no.
The ghetto was opened after we left.
As a matter of fact, I want you to know that my city had a very large population of ethnic Germans who identified with Germany.
And I think they prepared the ground very well for the German occupation forces.
You know, they were all traitors, all these Germans who lived in the city of Lodz.
And then, in November, the very beginning of November, we lived upstairs on the second floor.
My mother, I remember distinctly-- she went downstairs, because our janitor downstairs was sick.
And he asked my mother to come down and see what's wrong with him.
She wasn't home.
I was alone, and there was a knock on the door, or a doorbell rang-- I don't remember.
I opened the door, and there were three Germans-- one in a green uniform-- the Schutzpolizei, or whatever-- and two in black uniforms of the so-called Volksdeutschen, who I described before.
Those were the ethnic Germans who right away took over, you know-- they helped the occupation forces right away.
And they came in.
And they said that-- where is my mother?
I said, my mother is downstairs.
In the meantime, she probably-- people heard some commotion and let her know.
And I-- she came upstairs.
And they said, you are arrested for political reasons.
And, you know-- and the term-- the term deportation was not known in those days.
This was-- there was no precedent.
We were the first ones.
We didn't know.
We really thought we were arrested-- for what reasons, we didn't know.
And the Germans said, put all your valuables on the table.
Put a few warm things.
And-- and, by the way, they said that I was not on the list.
And my mother said, oh, go to your boyfriend, you know?
I had a boyfriend at that time.
You aren't on the list.
Just-- just-- just go.
And I didn't want to leave.
I was 19 years old.
I wanted to be with my mother.
I didn't want to leave her and go anywhere.
So anyway, my mother-- we started to prepare the few warm things to take along with us.
And my mother-- in those days, already, this was the beginning of the war.
So, just in case of emergency, she was carrying her jewelry in the little bundle, like a little sack, you know?
And she said to me, throw it in the toilet.
And I, as an obedient-- because they said, if you don't leave all the valuables on the table, you will be shot.
You know?
And my mother told me to throw it in the toilet, which I did.
And I flushed the water-- the whole-- you know, like an obedient daughter.
And then they-- we went.
They sealed our apartment.
And they took us to a gathering spot, which was my old high school.
And there I see that we are not the only ones.
There are maybe 1,000 people, you know--
--gentiles and Jews, alike.
Well, we--
--we lived always in a mixed neighborhood.
There were areas, like in every city of Poland, and I suppose-- suppose of other European countries, that Jews tended to live, you know.
But we lived in a mixed area.
High school, well, they-- this was-- that's what we found later-- that this was a list by name of the people.
They took the Polish Catholics as well as Polish Jews.
Who prepared that list?
I don't know.
Apparently, the Germans, when they came to a city and occupied the city, they had a procedure.
They were afraid of resistance.
So, first of all, they took care of the people whom they suspected might give them problems, you know.
So I saw there a whole-- many of my teachers, many of my parents' friends, doctors, and-- and-- and local merchants and-- whoever you want, you know?
It was-- in that transport, I think it was about 1,000-- over 1,000 people, if I remember correctly.
And they put us on passenger trains-- not on cattle trains, on passenger trains.
And we don't know where we going.
We have no money.
We have no jewelry.
We don't know where we're going So part--
Oh, definitely, of course!
We knew it right away.
This was their first-- you know, they-- they-- this is the occupying forces, and they-- this was how they behaved.
Absolutely!
We felt it also.
And because we had to wear right away the yellow armbands.
So we knew it was anti-Jewish.
I know, but you saw also a lot of Catholics there.
The Catholics were also.
You see, the Polish Catholics-- Poland was never a collaborationist country like some of the other European countries.
You know, Polish people were extremely-- I'm talking about the Polish Catholics as well as the Jews-- extremely patriotic.
And-- and the Germans were afraid of resistance.
And they took-- part of our transport went to the city of Czestochowa, and part of us went to Krakow.
I think-- I don't know about Czestochowa, but from Lodz it must have been about three, four hours.
Normal passenger car, you know.
And of course, we were, you know, crowded there, and everything else.
And we arrived in Krakow.
I don't know how they did it with the other group.
I don't remember.
But our group arrived in Krakow.
And we were put into an old Polish fortress in Krakow.
And, of course, straw-- and here we coming from those very nice apartment, with plenty of food, and-- and clothes.
And here we come-- straw on the floor-- cold.
This was-- this was November-- the beginning of November, and it's very cold in Poland--
Yeah, '39.
And as far as-- the Polish Catholics were registered by the Red Cross.
And we were received by the-- the representatives of the Jewish-- like here, Jewish Federation-- Jewish community-- force-- Jewish community center.
And we were supposed to register.
And as we were standing in line, my mother and I-- crying and being very upset-- a young man came to that line and ask us if we have a place to stay-- if we have family or friends in Krakow.
We didn't know no one-- anyone in Krakow.
And he said, don't worry, I take you to my house.
I have two empty apartments.
You will be with me.
And so it was.
First of all, we stood for hours outside, already in the snow, and the cold, and not dressed very-- very well, waiting until they were finished with their registration.
And then this young man came.
And I don't know if you know what a dorozka is.
You know what a dorozka is?
It's like a coach.
It's a horse-drawn carriage.
You know, in Poland, before the war, there were very few private cars.
We had dorozkas.
We had trolley cars.
So this was our transportations.
And this guy took two dorozkas, because he ask somebody else to come with him.
And I, my mother and I, were in one.
And he said to us that he has to stop in front of his place of work.
He still-- his brother and him still owned the business.
They had, like, a wholesale of leather skins, you know?
And he said he has to stop and let his brother know that they have guests.
And he stopped.
And who does he come back to the dorozka?
With my husband--
[LAUGHS] --future husband.
And I remember how he introduced us.
My friend-- high school professor-- this-- everybody in Poland was a professor-- [LAUGHS] --the teachers-- a high-school professor, Leopold Pfefferberg.
And this is how I met my husband-- yeah, my future husband.
And this guy took us to his house.
And my husband, and then other two people, lived also in this apartment, because my husband's parents were thrown out from their apartment by the Germans before I came-- before my mother and I came.
So part of the family-- he lived with this friend, and his parents and the sister lived with some family.
So the family was, you know, broken here.
No, no, no, no-- completely different section of the city.
No, no, no.
I just met him--
--in the dorozka here.
[LAUGHS]
No, future--
--I said future husband, yeah.
My future husband, again-- there were many doctors-- many-- and many people who fled toward the Russian-- you know, toward eastern part of Poland.
And from there, they were taken, or they fled themselves, deeper into Russia.
And my mother and I always felt very sorry that we didn't do it.
We started.
There was a big exodus in my city of Lodz, at the very beginning, right two days after the Germans came.
Everybody was trying to get to eastern Poland.
So there was a big exodus of the city-- everything by foot, you know?
And my mother and I packed little suitcases, and we went as far as the end of the city, you know?
And then my mother said, where are we going?
Where will we go?
You know, the two women alone-- and we came back.
And this was our biggest mistake.
Although you never knew what could have happened, because the Germans were shooting those people who were running away.
They were shooting them from airplanes, you know?
They are mowing them like-- and so-- where was I?
You were-- when you met, now, Poldek, and you were in their apartment.
Yeah.
And Poldek arranged it for my mother.
Many doctors left there for eastern Poland, leaving their offices, their equipment, and everything.
So Poldek rented it for my mother the complete office.
And she practiced from the very beginning, when she came, she practiced.
But we stayed for one-- for-- I think for one month or two months with this friend of ours.
And then my parents-- future parents-in-law-- got their apartment back-- completely empty, but they managed to get it back.
And the family moved again there.
And they asked us to move with them.
So.
And of course, the restrictions-- everybody had to work, because if we didn't have the work permit, we couldn't stay in the city.
We couldn't received our food allotment-- nothing.
That was already now in 1940s.
No, no, no.
I'm still talking about 1939.
And it went like this till about-- 1940.
I think in 1941, we went to the ghetto.
In the-- I married my husband.
We came in November.
In February of 1940, we got engaged.
And in 19-- in July of 1940, we got married.
Yeah.
So we still had a pretty nice wedding-- and with the only rabbi that was left in the city-- a very orthodox rabbi, you know?
And for the first time in my life, I saw, you know, the young couple staying under the chuppah, and the-- no-- the-- the bride has to go around her-- the groom seven times, or whatever, you know?
[LAUGHS]
So this was-- well, this was exactly the first wedding I attended, yeah.
I was still young.
But-- and then you started to-- you-- you tried to study in Krakow?
It was impossible.
The Jews could not-- were not accepted in universities.
And my-- my husband could not teach, because the Jewish schools were closed.
And he was not allowed to teach in the Polish schools.
Well, we were supposed to work.
I was working-- both of us, as a matter of fact.
At that particular time, we were working for the Jewish Federation in the office, you know.
And also what the Germans loved to do-- catch the people on the street to shovel snow.
They thought this is something very important, you know, that we had to do.
But mostly men did it, at the very beginning.
They didn't bother the woman that much yet.
Well, my mother was with us.
But then, in-- what was it-- November-- I think of September, maybe, of 1940, there was a new decree.
Whoever came after September 1, 1939, to the city of Krakow has to leave.
And we tried everything to use the connections that we had, possibly to allow my mother to stay.
I was married to Poldek already, so I could stay, but my mother-- they-- in those days, they left-- they let the people to choose the city or town where they wanted to go.
And my mother chose Warsaw.
This was all within the-- the so-called Generalgouvernement.
You couldn't go to the city of Lodz, because this was this Deutsches Reich proper.
This was Germany proper already.
And Generalgouvernement was the occupied territory.
So she could choose, and she chose to go to--
--Warsaw.
My mother was born in 1889.
So in 19-- when she died, when she was killed, she was 51.
She went to Warsaw.
She had no one in Warsaw.
She made some friends, of course.
And twice we were able, on a special permit, to visit her.
And going there, it was absolutely evident to us that this is a death place.
You know, the-- the ghetto was not even close.
This was the area on which they made the ghetto, later on.
In Warsaw.
The ghetto wasn't even close, but death was lurking from every corner.
People were already hungry, because there were thousands of people that the Germans threw out of the smaller towns, villages, cities.
And they all brought them to Warsaw.
And the people were already hungry.
They were not dressed.
The-- the-- the living conditions were terribly crowded.
The conditions were much worse than in Krakow, in that-- at that time.
And--
